coredata.dc:title: Silver nanosol SERS quantitative analysis of ultratrace biotin coupled N-doped carbon dots catalytic amplification with affinity reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Libing
Li, Chongning
Luo, Yanghe
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: Highly catalytic and stable N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared rapidly by microwave procedure using glucose as precursor and ammonium sulfite as N-dopant. The reduction of AgNO3 by trisodium citrate (TCA) was slow to form nanosilver (AgNP), and the N-CDs exhibited strong catalysis of the AgNP reaction. The formed AgNPs were used as indicator in the presence of Vitoria blue B (VBB) molecule probe with a SERS peak at 1615 cm−1. With the increase of nancatalyst N-CDs concentration, the AgNP reaction speed up, and the SERS peak of VBB enhanced linearly due to formation of more AgNPs as substrate. In the presence of avidin (Ad), the SERS peak weakened. Upon addition of biotin, the SERS peak enhanced due to turn on the indicator nanoreaction. The enhanced SERS signal had a good linear relationship with the biotin concentration in range of 0.0006–0.021 ng/mL, with a detection limit of 0.3 pg/mL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biotin
N-doped carbon dots
Nanocatalysis
Affinity reaction
SERS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by microwave procedure
SERS analytical principle of the CDs catalysis
SERS spectra of the CDs catalytic reaction
Ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence spectra of analytical system
Characterization of N-CDs by infrared, fluorescence and absorption spectra
Optimization of the VH analytical system
Working curve for SERS detection of VH
Influence of coexisting substances on the N-CDs catalytic SERS determination of VH
SERS analysis of VH in sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of nanosilver SERS detection of VH by coupled affinity reaction with catalytic amplification of CDN.

SERS spectra of the catalytic system. A: C0.7 catalytic system, the concentration of a to g is 0, 0.03, 0.06, 0.13, 0.15, 0.18 and 0.24 μg/mL C0.7 respectively. B: C0.7-Ad system, the concentration of a to g is 0, 0.03, 0.06, 0.12, 0.3, 0.45 and 0.6 ng/mL Ad respectively. C: VH analytical system, the concentration of a to g is 0, 0.00067, 0.002, 0.0033, 0.0047, 0.006 and 0.0067 ng/mL VH respectively.

Images of scanning electron microscope, transmission electron microscope and energy spectra. a: 0.93 mmol/L AgNO3 + 5.33 mmol/L TCA + 0.33 μmol/L VBB + 0.013 mol/L NaCl + 0.18 μg/mL C0.7 + 0.3 ng/mL Ad; b: a + 0.006 ng/mL VH; c: SEM of C0.7; d: TEM of C0.7; e: TEM of b.

Infrared, fluorescence and absorption spectra of C0.7 prepared by microwave irradiation. A: Infrared spectra of C0.7; B: fluorescence spectra of 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75 and 2 μg/mL C0.7; C: Absorption spectra of 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 25 and 30 μg/mL C0.7.
Comparison of CDs catalysis and Ad inhibition characteristics.
Analytical results for VH in milk.

head.ce:title: Silver nanosol SERS quantitative analysis of ultratrace biotin coupled N-doped carbon dots catalytic amplification with affinity reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Bioengineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Bioengineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y.H. Luo, School of Food and Bioengineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China.

Correspondence to: Z.L. Jiang, Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly catalytic and stable N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were prepared rapidly by microwave procedure using glucose as precursor and ammonium sulfite as N-dopant. The reduction of AgNO3 by trisodium citrate (TCA) was slow to form nanosilver (AgNP), and the N-CDs exhibited strong catalysis of the AgNP reaction. The formed AgNPs were used as indicator in the presence of Vitoria blue B (VBB) molecule probe with a SERS peak at 1615 cm−1. With the increase of nancatalyst N-CDs concentration, the AgNP reaction speed up, and the SERS peak of VBB enhanced linearly due to formation of more AgNPs as substrate. In the presence of avidin (Ad), the SERS peak weakened. Upon addition of biotin, the SERS peak enhanced due to turn on the indicator nanoreaction. The enhanced SERS signal had a good linear relationship with the biotin concentration in range of 0.0006–0.021 ng/mL, with a detection limit of 0.3 pg/mL.

ce:section.ce:para: Biotin, also known as vitamin H (VH), was a water-soluble B-group of vitamins, which participated in the metabolism of protein, fat and carbohydrate. It was one of the essential vitamins for animal organism to maintain normal physiological function, and was widely used in food, medicine, feed, fermentation and other fields. Methods for VH included microbial (Wang, 2013), high pressure liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Liu, Zhou, & Tian, 2008), fluorescence (Reyes, Romero, & Luque de Castro, 2001), chemiluminescence (Traore & Su, 2012) and immunoassay (Lin, Chen, Liang, Liu, & Hou, 2019). A microbial assay was reported to detect 0.1–1 ng/mL biotin according to the characteristic of plant lactobacillus (Shi, Sheng, Zhang, & Wang, 2018). A HPLC with ultraviolet detector was used to detect VH in molasses (Liu et al., 2008). A fluorescence biosensor was fabricated, using biotin and streptavidiN-specific identification to determine 0.1–8.0 ng/mL VH (Reyes et al., 2001). Up to date, there were no reports about SERS determination of VH by carbon dots and affinity reaction.

Carbon dots (CDs) were a new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, with no heavy metal leakage, high fluorescence activity, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, and have been applied in many fields such as biological imaging and sensor (Liu et al., 2012; Li, Kang, Liu, & Lee, 2012). Hydrothermal method was a classical procedure for preparation of CDs that has been used in the fluorescence analysis (Lin et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2016; Liu, Li, Dong, Luo, & Li, 2016; Bourlinos et al., 2017; Du & Guo, 2016; Zhu et al., 2012; Li et al., 2014). The polyethylene imide functional CDs were prepared by hydrothermal procedure, and used to determine 10–1100 nmol/L Cu2+ by fluorescence method (Dong et al., 2012). Based on CDs electrochemical luminescence, it was utilized to determine 10 pg/L pentachlorophenol (PCP) (Li et al., 2013). Based on CDs catalytic colored reaction, 0.0010–0.50 mmol/L glucose was determined (Shi et al., 2011). The N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) showed strong catalysis of gold nanoparticles (AuNP) reaction between HAuCl4 and H2O2, which was used for surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) detection of 0.02–1.7 μmol/L SO4 2− (Wang, Li, Luo, Liang, & Jiang, 2018). Based on the Fe/N-CDs catalysis of a colored reaction, a 0.1–100 mmol/L H2O2 was detected (Yang et al., 2017). Microwave procedure was a simple and rapid for preparation of CDs (Yao, 2017; Xu & Chen, 2018). The CDs were synthesized by microwave pyrolysis, and utilized to determine 5.4 × 10−10 g/mL 2-methoxyestradiol by chemiluminescence (Yao, 2017). A N/S-CDs fluorescent probe prepared by microwave irradiation was reported for detection of 0.01–70 mg/L levofloxacin (Li et al., 2018). However, there were no reports about catalytic N-CDs prepared by microwave with glucose and ammonia sulfite, and detection of VH by the affinity-mediated N-CDs catalytic reaction with SERS detection.

BiotiN-avidin system (BAS) was a highly affinity biological reaction. Strong affinity and multi-level amplification effect make BAS immune-labeling and related tracer more sensitive. Using biotin and BODIPY dye labeling, a sensitive affinity fluorescence assay was developed to determine 0.2 nmol/L biotin (Song & Swanson, 2001). Using Fe3O4/Au nanoparticles as the carrier and VH labeled as antibodies, an ELISA assay was reported to detect hepatitis B surface antigen (HBSAg) (Yu, Geng, Fu, Chen, & Cui, 2007). An acoustic resonator biosensor was fabricated to detect 30–500 nmol/L MUC1, based on the streptavidin to capture VH (Guo et al., 2015). Based on multi-enzyme system and specific BAS, an affinity amplification enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay was established to determine 1 × 10−1–1 × 104 μg/L O,O-diethyl organophosphorus pesticides (Wang et al., 2011). Based on the affinity reaction and gold nanoparticle aggregation, a photometric detection of 0.01–0.2 μg/mL avidin was reported (Shi et al., 2018). An affinity peptide surface plasmon resonance assay was reported for detection of 1.89 ng/mL cholera toxin, based on a 2nd antibody labeled with Cy3 (Lim et al., 2018). SERS was a sensitive molecular spectral technique, and was combined with specific bio-reactions such as immune and affinity reactions (Han et al., 2011; Ma, Li, Gong, Jiang, & Huan, 2015). The affinity was higher than the immunoreactions, and has been used in SERS detection. A label-free indirect immune-SERS assay was reported for detection of 10−10-10−8 mol/L IgG, based on the biotiN-conjugated antibodies being attached to avidiN-aggregated Ag nanoparticles via VH–avidin biorecognition (Wen, Jing, Liang, & Jiang, 2018). An affinity SERS method was developed to detect 0.005–5 nmol/L DNA, using 5,5′ dithio-bis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) modified gold nanoparticles as reporter probes on the solid substrate (Kroh, 1994). Recently, a SERS quantitative analytical strategy was proposed for trace microalbuminuria based on immunorecognition and graphene oxide nanoribbon (GON) catalysis, in nanosol substrate (Campos et al., 2015). We have known that the size of CDs is smaller than the GON, the CDs stability in water solution and catalytic activity were better than the GON. Therefore, the development of selective affinity SERS platform with CDs catalytic amplification was important. To our best knowledge, there were no reports about CDs catalytic-affinity SERS method for VH. In this paper, we studied the nanocatalytic reaction by SERS and combined with an affinity reaction to detect ultratrace VH in milk.

A model of DXR smart Raman spectrometer (Thermo, USA) with laser wavelength of 633 nm, power of 3.5 mW and a slit of 25.0 μm, a model of TU-1901 double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkingje General Instrument Co., Ltd. China) were used to record the Raman and absorption spectra. The nanoreactions were carried out on HH-S2 electric hot water bath (Earth Automation Instrument Plant, Jintan, China). The characterizations of nanoparticles were carried out on S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan/Oxford Company, Oxford, UK). The laser scattering was carried out on Zeta sizer Nano particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Co., UK). The ultrapure water was obtained on SYZ-550 quartz sub-boiling distiller (Crystal Glass Instrument Plant, Jiangsu, Nanjing, China). The carbon dots were synthesized on WX-6000 microwave digestion instrument (Preekem Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

A 10 μmol/L VBB (CAS NO. 2580-56-5, analytical reagent-AR, Merck Co., USA), ammonium sulfate (CAS NO. 7783–20-2, AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), ammonium sulfite (CAS NO. 10196-04-0, AR, Shanghai Wokai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), trisodium citrate (CAS NO. 6132-04-3, AR, Shanghai Reagent Factory, Shanghai, China), AgNO3 (CAS NO. 7761-88-8, AR, Xi Long Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), glucose (CAS NO. 50-99-7, AR, Xi Long Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), biotin (Vitamin H, China, CAS NO. 58-85-5, Beijing Soleil Bao Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China), and avidin (Ad, CAS NO. 1405-69-2, Sigma Co., USA) were used. A 0.0064 mol/L AgNO3 working solution was freshly mixed 14 mL 0.01 mol/L AgNO3 and 8.0 mL of 0.1 mol/L trisodium citrate (TCA). A 7.14 μmol/L VBB probe solution was mixed 5.0 mL of 10 μmol/L VBB and 2.0 mL of 1 mol/L NaCl. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. All the solutions were prepared with double distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by microwave procedure
ce:section.ce:para: A 1 g glucose (Glu) was accurately weighed into four beakers containing 30 mL water, and 0 g, 0.4 g, 0.7 g, 1.0 g of ammonium sulfite were added respectively. Then, they were transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave substrate. The conditions for microwave preparation of N-CDs, such as pressure, temperature and irradiation time were examined. A microwave irradiation time of 10 min at pressure of 30 atm and temperature of 180 ℃ was chosen to obtain highly catalysis and stable N-CDs. After the microwave irradiation time of 10 min, it was cooled naturally to room temperature to obtain a brownish yellow clear solution. The solutions were numbered as C0, C0.4, C0.7, C1.0. The concentration (calculated by glucose) was 0.032 g/mL. It was adjusted to neutral with 0.1 mol/L NaOH, to obtain a concentration of 0.02 g/mL. The required concentration was diluted as needed. The 0.13 μg/mL C0.7 reagent solution was mixed 15 mL 0.17 μg/mL C0.7 and 5.0 mL 0.01 ng/mL Ad.

Into a 5 mL test tube, 200 μL 0.13 μg/mL C0.7 reagent solution, different volume of 0.01 ng/mL VH, 220 μL 0.0064 mol/L AgNO3 working solution were added, and it was diluted to 1.5 mL with water. After mixed, the tube was heated in a water bath at 80 °C for 20 min, the ice-water was used to stop the reaction. Then it was added 70 μL of 7.14 μmol/L VBB probe solution. The mixture was transferred into a 1 cm quartz cell, and the SERS spectra were recorded. The SERS peak intensity at 1615 cm−1 (I) was measured, the blank value (I0) was measured without VH. The value of ΔI = I − I0 was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS analytical principle of the CDs catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Caramel has been prepared by classical hydrothermal method (Kroh, 1994), using Glu and ammonium sulfite as precursor. We found it can be used as a catalyst for the AgNP nanoreaction. In this article, a facile and rapid microwave irradiation was used to prepare caramel, also called as N-CDs. Under the microwave irradiation, Glu reacted with ammonium sulfite to form strongly catalytic N-CDs. In the absence of the catalyst, the reduction of AgNO3 by TCA was slow. This was proved by SERS spectra with lowest signal, and scanning electron microscopy with less silver nanoparticles. When N-CDs catalyst was added, the reaction significantly enhanced with the N-CDs concentration increased. The SERS peak at 1615 cm−1 enhanced using VBB as molecular probe, owing to the generated AgNPs substrate as indicator increasing. When Ad was added, it inhibited the N-CDs catalytic action and the SERS peak weakened. When VH was added, a stable Ad-VH complex formed, and the catalytic activity recovered. As the VH concentration increased, the catalytic AgNPs reaction enhanced, and the SERS signal increased linearly. Thus, a selective and sensitive affinity SERS quantitative analysis method was developed for biotin, coupled with the N-CDs catalytic amplification (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectra of the CDs catalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: Vitoria blue B (VBB) is a sensitive SERS molecule probe and is needed to indicate the AgNPs concentration changing in the catalytic system. The SERS activity of CDs was very weak, and the catalytic application in SERS quantitative analysis was rare. In this article, the C0-C1.0 catalytic systems were considered by the SERS technique, they produced SERS peaks at 240 cm−1, 432 cm−1, 528 cm−1, 808 cm−1, 1202 cm−1, 1394 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1 that was ascribing to tensile vibration of CN and CC–CC. With C0-C1.0 concentration increased, the catalytic effect increased as a result of the silver nanoparticle substrate produced, and the SERS signal enhanced (Fig. 2 A, Figs. S1–S3). When Ad was added, Ad bound to C0-C1.0 to inhibit the AgNO3-TCA reaction, the weak SERS signal occurred due to formation of the less silver nanoparticles in the system (Fig. 2B, Figs. S4–S6). Upon addition of VH, stable Ad-VH complex and free CDN formed, that caused the SERS peak enhancing due to turn on the indicator nanoreaction. The strongest SERS peak was at 1615 cm−1 and it was selected for use. The increased SERS signal was linear with VH concentration (Figs. S7–S9). Among them, the C0.7 SERS was most sensitive due to its catalysis was strongest, and was chosen for assay of 0.0003–0.012 ng/mL VH with five parallel detections (Fig. 2C).

Under the experimental conditions, the redox reaction between Ag+ and TCA was very slow, that is to say, the redox electroN-transfer was difficult. Upon addition of CDs without doped-N (C0), it catalyzed the reduction of AgNO3 to produce AgNPs by TCA. With the increase of catalyst concentration, the ΔI increased due to the generated AgNPs increasing (Table 1 ). When the doped-N CDs including C0.4, C0.7 and C1.0 were used as catalyst respectively, the AgNP reactions enhnaced greatly. Among them, C0.7 has the strongest catalytic effect that depended on the large surface charges due to N atom being a donor atom. When the Ad was added, it adsorbed on the surface of the CDs by electrostatic action to inhibit the catalytic activity. As the amount of Ad increased in the range of 0.03–0.6 ng/mL Ad, the catalytic activity decreased and the SERS effect tended to decreasing (Fig. 2B). The decreased SERS value had a linear relationship with Ad concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence spectra of analytical system
ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet absorption spectra of the analytical system were recorded. Two absorption peaks generated at 220 nm and 290 nm for the VH analytical system. Within a concentration range of 3.33–66.6 ng/mL VH, as the concentration of VH increasing, the catalytic activity increased and the absorption peak increased gradually. This was because the silver nanoparticles generated by the catalysis (Fig. S10). Generally, silver nanosols exhibited surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak at about 400 nm and 500 nm (Lee et al., 2014). There were no SPR peaks at about 400 nm and 500 nm for the produced silver nanoparticles because the aggregation reagent of NaCl was added in the analytical system to obtain sensitive SERS effect. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of C0.7 were examined, it exhibited a fluorescence peak at 440 nm and a Rayleigh scattering peak at 355 nm with an excited wavelength of 355 nm (Fig. S11). We attempted to construct a fluorescent method for biotin coupled CDs fluorescent probes with the affinity reaction, but it was fail. The fluorescence spectra of C0.7-Ad system indicated that the peak decreased with the Ad increasing (Fig. S12), it was owing to the interaction between CDs of C0.7 and Ad. That is, the Ad was wrapped the fluorescence probe of C0.7 to cause the intensity decreasing. The fluorescence spectra of AgNO3-TCA–C0.7–Ad-VH system were also considered, but there was no obvious fluorescence change when biotin was increased, although high concentration of C0.7 was used (Fig. S13).

For the VH analytical system (Fig. 3 a, 3b), the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of silver nanoparticles was recorded, with a diameter of 75 nm and 50 nm for the blank and 0.006 ng/mL VH, respectively. Fig. 4 c was a SEM of C0.7 particles with a size of 20 nm, that contains 15.6% C (weight %) and 0.1% N. The energy spectra of the analytical system were also recorded by scanning electron microscopy with a voltage of 5.0 kV. Results indicated that the catalytic system exhibited a silver peak at 3 keV, the peak at 0.3 keV for N element ascribing to C0.7, and the Si peak at 1.8 keV was ascribing to the electron microscope padded silicon wafer. The TEMs of C0.7 (Fig. 3d) and AgNPs in the analytical system (Fig. 3e) show that the particle size was 18 nm and 60 nm respectively.

A nanoparticle size analyzer was used to record the size distribution of the VH analytical system. It can be seen from the results (Fig. S14) that there were four peaks at 1.5–6 nm, 6–18 nm, 18–295 nm, 295–3580 nm, with an average size of 95 nm when no VH was added. When the VH concentrations were 0.003 ng/mL, there were two peaks at 1.7–5.6 nm and 10–1480 nm, with an average diameter of 97 nm. When VH was 0.006 ng/mL, it exhibited three peaks at 1.5–6 nm, 6–530 nm and 530–6440 nm, with an average diameter of 100 nm. The particle size slowly increased due to formation more silver nanoparticles with strong N-CDs catalysis and its aggregation in the analytical system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs by infrared, fluorescence and absorption spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The molecular techniques including infrared, fluorescence, absorption and SERS were used to characterize the N-CDs. The carbon dots were prepared according to the experimental procedure. A 30 mL of the N-CDs solution was dialysis for 24 h with 1000 Dalton molecular weight, and was placed in a vacuum freeze dryer, and the obtained powder sample was recorded by infrared spectrometer. From the infrared spectra (Fig. 4A), it was found that the stretching vibration of NH in C0.7 was at 3445.40 cm−1, the peaks at 1636.12, 1140.97, 1194.86 and 1507 cm−1 were ascribing to CC double bond stretching vibration. The peaks at 666.54 and 616.05 cm−1 may be ascribing to CH bending vibration (Campos et al., 2015). The carbon dots C0.7 exhibited a strong fluorescence peak at 395 nm when the excited wavelength was at 320 nm, and the peak increased linearly with C0.7 concentration increasing (Fig. 4B). In addition, a Rayleigh scattering peak at 320 nm and a weak 1/2 frequency scattering peak at 640 nm were recorded. The C0.7 was in yellow brown and appeared at absorption peak at 280 nm (Fig. 4C). No Raman peaks were observed for the N-CDs solid, and the N-CDs solution using AgNPs as substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the VH analytical system
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction conditions of VH analytical system were optimized. When the AgNO3 concentration was 0.93 mmol/L, TCA concentration was 5.33 mmol/L, the system ΔI achieved the maximum value due to formation of largest number of silver nanoparticles, so the AgNO3 and TCA centration was selected as 0.93 mmol/L and 5.33 mmol/l (Fig. S15a-b). While carbon dots C0, C0.4, C1.0, C0.7 concentrations were 42.64 μg/mL, 0.2 μg/mL, 0.51 mg/mL and 0.18 μg/mL, the system ΔI achieved the maximum value due to its strongest catalysis, so the concentration was selected as the optimal concentration (Fig. S15c, Fig. S16-S18). When Ad was 3 ng/mL, the ΔI was maximal, and a 0. 3 ng/mL Ad was selected (Fig. S15d). The AgNP reaction temperature was 80 ℃, the ΔI achieved the maximum value (Fig. S15e), so the reaction temperature of 80 ℃ was chosen for use. When reaction time was 20 min, ΔI achieved the maximum value (Fig. S15f), so the reaction time was selected. When NaCl was 0.02 mol/L, the ΔI achieved the maximum value due to formation of largest silver nanoparticle aggregations, so the NaCl concentration was selected (Fig. S15g). Molecular probe of VBB was 0.33 μmol/L, the ΔI was the maximal value because it adsorbed most on the surface of silver nanoparticles, and was chosen for use (Fig. S15h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working curve for SERS detection of VH
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions of the VH analytical system, working curves (Fig. S19) for VH with different concentrations (C) and corresponding ΔI were obtained, and the analytical features show in Table 2 . From the results, the slope of the working curve of the C0.7 catalytic SERS system was the largest, its sensitivity was the highest with a Linear equation of ΔI = 61331C + 95.5 in the linear range of 0.0006–0.021, and a detection limit (DL) of 0.0003 ng/mL VH, and the system of C0.7 was selected to detect biotin in real sample. Compared with the reported method (Table S1) (Wang, 2013; Liu et al., 2008; Reyes et al., 2001; Traore & Su, 2012; Lin et al., 2019), this SERS quantitative method was simple to operate and has high sensitivity for rapid detection of VH. In addition, the N-CDs were very stable, and the time of preparation CDs by microwave procedure was very short.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of coexisting substances on the N-CDs catalytic SERS determination of VH
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of coexisting substances on the SERS determination of 0.0067 ng/mL VH was investigated by the analytical system procedure. Results demonstrated that 0.67 ng/ml Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, HSA, alanine, histidine and aspartic acid, 0.34 ng/ml tryptophan, tyrosine, vitamin B1, threonine, vitamin K3, glutamate and lysine, 0.13 ng/ml Cr3+, Fe3+, dopamine and BSA did not interfered with the determination (Table S2). This demonstrated this SERS quantitative analytical method had good selectivity due to the highly selective affinity reaction and the N-CDs catalytic amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS analysis of VH in sample
ce:section.ce:para: VH is a relatively stable water-soluble vitamin, it is an important component of carboxylase, and an indispensable substance for normal metabolism of fat and protein. It plays an important role in maintaining the normal physiological function of the body. Long-term deficiency of VH in vivo can cause diseases in immunity, metabolism and gene expression. It was commonly added in milk. Three milks were purchased from the supermarket, a suitable amount of sample was weighted into a beaker containing 50 mL water, it was irradiated by ultrasonic wave for 30 min, filtered to obtain the sample solution, and 1 mL the solution was used for detection. The blank was done, and measured by the SERS detection procedure in Section 2.2. The results are shown in Table 2, this SERS results were agreement with the reference results of chemiluminescence assay (Traore & Su, 2012), with a recovery of 97%–107%.

Two common reagents of glucose and ammonium sulfite were used to prepare stable N-CDs by microwave procedure, with rapidity only irradiation time of 10 min. The N-CDs exhibited strongly catalytic effect on the reaction of AgNO3-TCA to form the production of AgNP with high SERS activity in the presence of label-free molecular probes of VBB. Coupled with the highly specific affinity reaction, a facile nanosol SERS quantitative analysis method for VH was established. The method was simple, selective and sensitive, and has been used to determine the content of VH in samples because it was commonly added in milk.

Libing Wang: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Resources. Chongning Li: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Resources. Yanghe Luo: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision. Zhiliang Jiang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R and D Program of China [grant number 2018YFD0901003], and National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21767004, 21667006].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126433.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bourlinos et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots-Highly efficient photoluminescence redox catalyst for hydrogenations of olefins and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide
Applied Materials Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bourlinos, A.B., Rathi, A.K., Gawande, M.B., Hola, K., Goswami, A., Kalytchuk, S., Karakassides, M.A., Kouloumpis, A., Gournis, D., Deligiannakis, Y., Giannelis, E.P., & Zboril, R. (2017). Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots-Highly efficient photoluminescence redox catalyst for hydrogenations of olefins and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide. Appl. Mater. Today, 7, 179-184.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent chemosensor for pyridine based on N-doped carbon dots
